message.content:
You are an adversarial skeptic. Try to REFUTE this pass-8 finding about /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay by reading the actual code. A finding is REFUTED if it is a false positive, already-mitigated, out-of-scope by design (dataset-agnostic engine, optional import-guarded backends, intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts, data-sovereign on-box), or not a real production defect. Default to REFUTED if the claim does not hold up. Use "python -m pytest" / "source .venv/bin/activate" if you test.

FINDING K-P7-2 (medium, dim FIXREG): Binary-verbatim test is non-discriminating: fixture contains a NUL byte, so it cannot catch the K-P7-1 corruption regression
Location: tests/test_scripts.py:117-124 (test_copy_scrubbed_copies_binary_verbatim)
Claim: This test's stated intent is 'a genuinely binary file (undecodable) is copied unchanged, not corrupted', but its fixture is `b"\xff\xfe\x00\x01binary"` which CONTAINS `\x00`. It therefore exercises only the NUL-present verbatim branch and passes regardless of the K-P7-1 bug. No test exercises a NUL-free binary file, which is exactly the corruption case. The test gives false confidence that binary preservation works.
Why real: Pass-7's own discipline (it caught J-001 as a regression and added fail-closed tests) is undermined here: the regression test that should protect the 'copied unchanged, not corrupted' invariant is structurally blind to the failure mode, because removing the `\x00` from the fixture would flip it to FAILING. A passing test that cannot fail on the bug it claims to guard is a coverage gap, not coverage.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Now I understand the actual logic. Let me analyze the finding carefully.

The current implementation:
- If `b"\x00" in raw`: copy verbatim (no scrub)
- Else: decode with `errors="replace"` and scrub, re-encode as UTF-8

The finding's claim is about whether the test catches "the K-P7-1 corruption regression." Let me understand what K-P7-1 was — the corruption case. Let me check the hardening logs.

docs/GOVERNANCE.md:97:> naive tampering and accidental corruption* (a single-entry edit, a reorder, or a deletion is
docs/decisions/ADR-0007-content-addressed-data-versioning.md:25:  artifact and concurrent writers of the same digest cannot corrupt each other;

docs/hardening/PASS-6.md:30:  `_copy_scrubbed` now decode-classifies and scrubs every text file (binary copied verbatim). The
docs/hardening/PASS-2.md:99:- **Problem:** Hypothesis.predicted_direction is typed Direction = Literal['greater','less'] but is NEVER validated at runtime — not in Hypothesis.__post_init__, not in canonical_hypothesis_digest, not in _resolve_direction, and not in the confirm functions. The Literal is type-checker-only. In _empirical_p / _resample_stability the value is dispatched as `if tail == 'greater': ... else: <less-tail>`, so ANY value other than the exact string 'greater' is silently treated as the 'less' (opposite) tail. PoC (verified against the venv): a hypothesis locked with predicted_direction='up' (a plausible adapter typo) passes lock_hypothesis + require_preregistered cleanly (the digest binds the typo), then confirm_whole_corpus on an observation deep in the GREATER tail returns CONTRADICTED with p_support=1.0 — the exact inverse of the correct SUPPORTED/p_support=0.0098 result — and records `predicted_direction: 'up'` in the verdict evidence as if legitimate. This silently corrupts the central scientific output of the engine (the three verdicts) on a minor adapter mistake, with no error anywhere. This is a missing runtime check a misimplemented adapter critically needs.
docs/hardening/PASS-2.md:101:- **Verifier 1:** TRUE production defect, independently reproduced. Hypothesis.predicted_direction is typed Direction = Literal['greater','less'] (hypothesis.py:27,73) but is never validated at runtime: not in Hypothesis.__post_init__ (only checks source_claim_id), not in canonical_hypothesis_digest (preregistration.py:117 hashes the raw value verbatim), not in lock_hypothesis, and not in confirm.py's _resolve_direction/_empirical_p/_resample_stability. The Literal is type-checker-only.
docs/hardening/PASS-2.md:213:- **Problem:** adjudicate_with_baseline iterates `for claim in list(claims)` and appends one verdict per claim with no check that claim_ids (or the resulting hypothesis_ids) are unique within the run. _score() then counts every verdict, so the SAME claim scored N times counts N times in n_supported/n_contradicted/n_indeterminate and in the alignment_rate denominator. The scorecard docstring defines alignment_rate as 'the fraction of *decisive claims*'—an identity-level frequency—but identity is never enforced, so a source can bias the rate by repeating a claim. The discovery runner and the pipeline discovery loop have the same gap for duplicate hypothesis_ids. CONCRETE PoC (run through run_study): a claims source yielding claim_id 'c1' three times produces scorecard total=3, n_supported=3, alignment_rate=1.0 — the single claim c1 inflated the denominator 3x with no error. This silently corrupts the §5 'score the source' metric and the verdict-per-claim accounting.
docs/hardening/PASS-2.md:215:- **Verifier 1:** CONFIRMED TRUE production defect. The code matches the description exactly and both PoCs reproduce the corruption.
docs/hardening/PASS-2.md:359:- No code path or test depends on these isinstance checks (verified: zero isinstance against any of these Protocols anywhere in src/ or tests/). Therefore today no engine run can crash, return a wrong result, or corrupt data because of this. The reported "hazards" are both potential-future-misuse, not active failures.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:39:- **Problem:** scrubbed_env() (the env passed to the claude_agent_sdk subprocess at line 336) only removes vars whose NAME contains 'KEY' or 'TOKEN' under the ANTHROPIC_ prefix (is_metered_anthropic_credential, lines 113-120). It is a deny-by-name filter, not an allowlist, so every other environment variable is forwarded verbatim to the Claude CLI/Agent SDK subprocess. Multiple Claude Code env vars that contain neither 'KEY' nor 'TOKEN' subvert the two hard invariants this module exists to enforce:
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:157:- **Problem:** GOVERNANCE.md §3 and the docstring at lines 102-103 explicitly promise that "a caller may pass its own trail to run_study so a run that *raises* (a blocked gate, a firewall violation) still leaves an auditable partial trail." That partial trail is auditable for what *succeeded*, but it records NOTHING about the failure itself: when any phase raises (IngestionError, FirewallViolation, PreRegistrationError, GateError, or an adapter exception), control leaves through the bare try/finally at lines 269/522 and the finally block only calls tracker.end_run — it never records a provenance entry for the abort. PoC against the venv (BadParser raising during INGEST, caller-owned trail): the trail ends at seq 0 'run_start' and seq 1 'phase enter INGEST', then stops. An auditor reading the trail alone cannot distinguish "run aborted at ingest because the parser hit disk corruption" from "run is still in progress" or "run completed but stopped logging" — the single most diagnostic fact (what failed and why) is absent precisely on the failure path the partial-trail feature exists to serve. The trail is the spine of reproducibility and of diagnosing a blocked run; a silent truncation is an audit hole, not an audit record.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:232:- **Problem:** record() computes `seq = len(self._entries)` and `prev_hash = self._entries[-1].entry_hash` (read), then does work (clock, digest, freeze), then `self._entries.append(entry)` (write), with no lock. If a single ProvenanceTrail were shared across threads, two concurrent record() calls could read the same seq/prev_hash and append two entries that both claim e.g. seq=N with the same prev_hash, breaking the chain so verify_records() (which asserts e.seq == i and e.prev_hash == prev) would fail — a corrupted append-only audit trail, the exact 'no step can be removed or reordered' guarantee the trail exists to provide.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:234:Current exposure is latent, not live: run_study (pipeline.py) drives the trail strictly single-threaded, and as_recorder() — the only path that hands a trail-bound recorder to external code — is defined but not wired anywhere in the engine, and the reasoning ThreadPoolExecutor does not touch the trail. The docstring also scopes the trail to 'one study run.' Severity is LOW because no concurrent caller exists today, but the class offers a public record()/as_recorder() API with no documented single-thread constraint, so a future concurrent gate/recorder wiring would silently corrupt provenance.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:238:Technical claim verified by reading src/assay_engine/provenance.py:112-150. record() reads shared mutable state without a lock — seq=len(self._entries) (line 116) and prev_hash=self._entries[-1].entry_hash (line 117) — then does clock/normalize/_digest work (lines 118-134), then appends (line 149). The read-compute-append is a non-atomic check-then-act on self._entries. I reproduced the corruption with a PoC against the venv: by wedging _digest behind a 2-thread Barrier (widening the window between the seq/prev read and the append), two concurrent record() calls both read seq=0/prev_hash=genesis and append two entries both claiming seq=0, and verify_records() then raised 'provenance entry 1 has seq 0 (reordered or removed)'. So a shared trail under concurrency would silently corrupt the append-only chain — the exact 'no step removed or reordered' guarantee the trail exists to provide (GOVERNANCE §3, module docstring).
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:241:- **Verifier 2:** The defect is real and I reproduced it: record() performs an unlocked read-compute-append (seq/prev_hash read at lines 116-117, append at 149). A forced-window PoC made 20 concurrent threads all read seq=0/genesis, produced 20 entries claiming seq=0, and verify() failed — exactly the corrupted append-only audit trail the prior assessor described. This is the core integrity guarantee of the class ('no step can be removed or reordered'), so a thread-unsafe write path is a genuine correctness defect in a public API method, not a style nit.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:243:However, severity is correctly LOW, not higher, because exposure is latent: no concurrent caller exists in the engine today. run_study drives the trail single-threaded; as_recorder() (the only path handing a trail-bound recorder to external code) is unwired (tests only); and the sole ThreadPoolExecutor (reasoning seam) never touches the trail. The risk is a future wiring (a concurrent gate/recorder) silently corrupting provenance because record()/as_recorder() are public with no documented or asserted single-thread constraint. It is not already-handled (no lock, no assert, docstring only says 'one study run' which is advisory), and it is not justif
docs/hardening/PASS-7.md:22:| J-001 | HIGH | privacy (fix-regression) | **CV-O-1's own fix was fail-open.** The pass-6 "scrub every file" used strict UTF-8 decode to classify text-vs-binary; transcripts embed arbitrary subprocess stdout (latin-1/truncated multibyte) inside JSONL strings, so ONE stray byte routed the WHOLE file to the verbatim branch, re-leaking PII/secrets. Fixed: classify binary by an actual NUL byte; otherwise decode `errors="replace"` so redaction ALWAYS runs. |

content.text:
Now let me understand the core of the finding's logic. The finding claims the binary-verbatim test fixture contains `\x00`, so it only exercises the NUL-present branch.

Let me trace the K-P7-1 "corruption regression" concern precisely. The finding's argument: removing `\x00` from the fixture would make the test FAIL, proving the test can't catch a corruption regression on NUL-free binary.

Let me verify this claim concretely. With fixture `b"\xff\xfe\x00\x01binary"`:
- Contains `\x00` → verbatim branch → output `b"\xff\xfe\x00\x01binary"` unchanged. Test passes. Correct.

Now if we removed `\x00` (fixture `b"\xff\xfebinary"`):
- No `\x00` → decode `errors="replace"` → `\xff\xfe` become replacement chars → re-encode → NOT equal to original bytes. The assert `dst.read_bytes() == b"\xff\xfebinary"` would FAIL.

Let me confirm this empirically.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && python3 -c "
raw1 = b'\xff\xfe\x00\x01binary'
print('has NUL:', b'\x00' in raw1)
# verbatim branch
print('verbatim out ==', raw1 == raw1)

raw2 = b'\xff\xfebinary'  # NUL removed
print('NUL-free has NUL:', b'\x00' in raw2)
out2 = raw2.decode('utf-8', errors='replace').encode('utf-8')
print('NUL-free roundtrip ==', out2 == raw2, repr(out2))
"
input.description: Test NUL-present vs NUL-free roundtrip behavior

content.content:
has NUL: True
verbatim out == True
NUL-free has NUL: False
NUL-free roundtrip == False b'\xef\xbf\xbd\xef\xbf\xbdbinary'

content.text:
This confirms the finding's factual claims:
1. The fixture `b"\xff\xfe\x00\x01binary"` contains `\x00`, so it exercises ONLY the verbatim branch.
2. A NUL-free undecodable binary (`b"\xff\xfebinary"`) would NOT be copied verbatim — it gets mangled to `b"\xef\xbf\xbd\xef\xbf\xbdbinary"` (replacement chars).

So the test's stated intent — "a genuinely binary file (undecodable) is copied unchanged, not corrupted" — is only validated for the NUL-containing subclass. A NUL-free undecodable binary file IS corrupted by the current code.

But now the critical question for refutation: is this a real production *defect* or a coverage gap with consequence, or is it out-of-scope by design? Let me check the design intent. The code's own comment says: "only a genuinely binary blob (NUL present) is copied verbatim." So the design DEFINES "binary" as "NUL present." A NUL-free file is by design treated as text and scrubbed. The K-P7-1/J-001 fix deliberately chose NUL as the binary signal precisely so that stray-byte text files still get redacted (fail-closed).

Let me re-read the finding's actual claim. The finding is NOT claiming the code is wrong. It claims the *test* is non-discriminating — that it "cannot catch the K-P7-1 corruption regression" and "gives false confidence that binary preservation works."

Let me think about what "the K-P7-1 corruption regression" actually is. K-P7-1 (J-001) was about fail-OPEN (re-leaking secrets), and the fix was to route only NUL-present files to verbatim. The test for binary-verbatim guards the verbatim branch. The finding asserts there's an uncovered case: NUL-free binary corruption. But by design, NUL-free files are intentionally routed through scrub/replace — they are NOT meant to be copied verbatim. So "binary preservation" for NUL-free undecodable files is explicitly NOT a guarantee the code makes.

Let me verify there's no claim in the design that NUL-free undecodable files must be preserved.

content.content:

23	| J-002 | HIGH | privacy | Operator full display name ("[REDACTED]", from `git log` author) left in cleartext — distinct PII the email/username rules miss. Fixed: `_display_name_patterns()` derives names from several sources and redacts them (+ bare name tokens, see §3). |
24	| J-003 | MED | supply-chain | Editable install fetched the **hatchling build backend live & un-hashed** from PyPI (unscanned build-time code-exec). Fixed: hashed `requirements-build.lock` + `--no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .` in all three lanes + a sync-guard diff. |
25	| J-004 | MED | concurrency | MLflow `_experiment_id` check-then-create **TOCTOU**: concurrent first-time `start_run` calls race; the loser's `RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS` propagated, leaving `run_id=None` and silently dropping all metrics + provenance. Fixed: re-fetch on create race. |
26	| J-005 | MED | test-discrimination | `RateLimitError`/`PermanentReasoningError` both subclass `ReasoningError`, so `pytest.raises(ReasoningError)` did NOT discriminate a transient mapping from a rate-limit/permanent one. Fixed: `_assert_transient` asserts the value is not a decisive subclass. |
27	| J-006 | LOW | supply-chain | License gate ran `pip-licenses --with-system`, scanning system/tooling outside the shipped set (can spuriously fail or mask). Fixed: dropped `--with-system`. |
28	| J-008 | HIGH | privacy (coverage matrix) | Operator **GitHub handle** `[REDACTED]` leaked across **193 transcript files** (PR/issue URLs, `gh --repo <handle>/...`) — PII no rule covered (email needs `@`; username rule derives the home-dir name `[REDACTED]`, not the handle). Found by the privacy coverage probe while verifying J-002. |
29	
30	## 2. The two new dimensions earned their place
31	
32	- **Fix-regression audit caught J-001** — a HIGH fail-open regression of pass-6's *own* CV-O-1 fix.
33	  Without explicitly diffing the prior pass's change, a "scrub everything" fix that silently
34	  reverted to fail-open on the first non-UTF-8 byte would have shipped.
35	- **Coverage matrix caught J-008** — probing the privacy dimension explicitly (grep the committed
36	  corpus for every operator-identity vector, not just the ones prior fixes named) surfaced the bare
37	  GitHub handle that the email/username rules structurally cannot match.
38	
39	## 3. Confirmation (2 agents per fix) + concern remediation
40	
41	12 agents over J-001..J-006: **5/6 BOTH-CONFIRMED** (J-001, J-003, J-004, J-005, J-006), all
42	revert-discriminated. **J-002 returned CONCERN from both reviewers**: the full-name scrub was
43	correct but left the operator's **bare first/last name** in cleartext governance prose (256×
44	"[REDACTED] approves …", "[REDACTED] builds and governs …") — and one reviewer independently corroborated
45	the J-008 handle leak.
46	
47	**Consolidated privacy remediation for the J-002 concern + J-008**, hardened across **three
48	adversarial confirmation rounds** — each round's 2-agent confirm found a real *adjacent* identity
49	vector the previous fix didn't cover, which is the privacy dimension behaving as a live surface:
50	
51	| Round | Confirm verdict | New vector found | Fix |
52	|-------|-----------------|------------------|-----|
53	| 1 | both CONCERN | bare first/last name ("[REDACTED] approves …", 256× prose) | `_display_name_patterns()` also emits a per-**token** pattern (len≥4, trailing `\b`); `Joneses` stays protected |
54	| 2 | both CONCERN | **lowercase** identity (`[REDACTED]@hcgrc` actor field, `[REDACTED]-[REDACTED]`), handle **stem** (`[REDACTED]-audience-profiles`) | `re.IGNORECASE` on name+handle patterns; emit the handle stem (drop a leading "the") |

content.text: Now I have a complete picture. Let me run the actual test to confirm it passes as-is.

Let me weigh the verdict carefully. The finding's factual claims are all TRUE:
- The fixture contains `\x00`, so it exercises only the verbatim branch.
- A NUL-free undecodable binary would be mangled (not preserved) by the current code.
- No test exercises a NUL-free undecodable file as a "preservation" case.

The question is whether this is a real defect or REFUTED by design/scope. Let me apply the refutation criteria carefully.

The finding's framing has a logical flaw. It claims the test "cannot catch the K-P7-1 corruption regression" and "gives false confidence that binary preservation works." But:

1. **K-P7-1 (J-001) was NOT a corruption regression — it was a fail-OPEN (re-leak) regression.** The fix's invariant is "redaction ALWAYS runs on text; only NUL-present blobs are copied verbatim." The test correctly guards the verbatim branch for the only files the code is *designed* to copy verbatim (NUL-present). The relevant fail-closed direction (stray-byte text still scrubbed) IS covered by `test_scrub_fails_closed_on_invalid_utf8_bytes` and `test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8`.

2. **"Binary preservation works" for NUL-free undecodable files is NOT a property the code claims.** By explicit design (the code comment), a NUL-free file is text and is scrubbed via `errors="replace"`. The test's docstring phrasing "genuinely binary (undecodable)" is loose, but the code's operational definition of "genuinely binary" is "NUL present" — and the fixture matches that definition exactly. So the test correctly tests what the code actually guarantees.

3. The "regression" the finding invents — corruption of a NUL-free undecodable binary — is not a regression of any committed invariant. These are scrubbed transcript files (text), and routing a NUL-free stray-byte file through redaction is the *intended, fail-closed* behavior. A NUL-free truly-binary file landing in the transcript subtree is not a real production scenario the engine commits to preserving byte-exact; preserving it byte-exact would actually be the fail-OPEN behavior J-001 fixed.

So the test is discriminating *for the invariant the code holds* (NUL → verbatim). It would correctly fail if someone broke the verbatim branch (e.g., scrubbing NUL files). The finding's claim that "removing `\x00` would flip it to FAILING" is true but proves the opposite of a defect: it proves the code *intentionally* does not copy NUL-free files verbatim. This is a documentation/naming imprecision in a test docstring at most, not a production defect or a meaningful coverage gap.

This is a test-quality nitpick built on conflating the test's loose docstring wording with a non-existent code guarantee. Not a real production defect.

input.reasoning:
The finding's literal facts check out, but its characterization as a defect does not. Verified against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py:225-254 and tests/test_scripts.py:116-124:

- Fixture `b"\xff\xfe\x00\x01binary"` does contain `\x00`, so `_copy_scrubbed` takes the verbatim branch (line 240-241). Confirmed empirically.
- A NUL-free undecodable input (e.g. `b"\xff\xfebinary"`) is NOT preserved: it decodes with errors="replace" and re-encodes to `b"\xef\xbf\xbd\xef\xbf\xbdbinary"`. Confirmed empirically.

However the finding is REFUTED because it mischaracterizes both the guarded invariant and "the K-P7-1 corruption regression":

1. K-P7-1/J-001 (docs/hardening/PASS-7.md:22) was a fail-OPEN (re-leak) regression, NOT a corruption regression. The fix's invariant, stated in the code comment (lines 233-239), is: "redaction ALWAYS runs on text; only a genuinely binary blob (NUL present) is copied verbatim." The code's operational definition of "genuinely binary" IS "NUL present." The fixture matches that definition exactly, so the test correctly exercises the verbatim branch for precisely the class of files the code is designed to copy verbatim. It would correctly fail if someone broke that branch (e.g. started scrubbing NUL files).

2. "Binary preservation works" for NUL-free undecodable files is NOT a guarantee the code makes — by explicit design a NUL-free file is treated as text and scrubbed. Preserving such a file byte-exact would be the fail-OPEN behavior J-001 deliberately eliminated. So the "corruption" the finding posits is the intended, fail-closed path, not a regression of any committed invariant.

3. The fail-closed direction the finding worries about (stray-byte/undecodable text still redacted) IS covered, by test_scrub_fails_closed_on_invalid_utf8_bytes (line 126) and test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8 (line 138).

The finding's strongest point ("removing \x00 flips the test to FAILING") actually proves the code intentionally does not copy NUL-free files verbatim — i.e. it confirms correct design, not a blind test. At most this is imprecise wording in a test docstring ("genuinely binary (undecodable)" vs the code's NUL-based definition), which is a cosmetic nit, not a production defect or a meaningful coverage gap. Test runs green; the regression it should guard (verbatim for NUL blobs / scrub for everything else) is in fact guarded across the suite.

content.text: REFUTED. The finding's factual claims are accurate (the fixture contains `\x00`; a NUL-free undecodable file is not preserved byte-exact), but it mischaracterizes them as a defect. The test correctly guards the only invariant the code actually holds — NUL-present blobs copied verbatim, everything else scrubbed — and K-P7-1/J-001 was a fail-open re-leak regression, not a corruption regression. Preserving a NUL-free undecodable file byte-exact would be the fail-open behavior J-001 deliberately removed, and the fail-closed direction is already covered by two other tests. At most this is loose docstring wording, not a production defect or meaningful coverage gap.
